label: verify-54952-6
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Gov. Code § 54952.6
- § 54952.6
- §54952.6

## How the documents used it

[tetlock] …dditional remedial steps (recusal, disclosure, restart with new AI configuration) to be legally sufficient? (P=15)
- §54960.1's void/cure remedy is keyed to 'action taken' (a collective decision, commitment, or vote per §54952.6) in violation of enumerated sections (54953, 54954.2, 54954.5, 54954.6, 54956, 54956.5). Because no vote on Harbor Yards has occurred yet, there is arguably no ripe 'action' for a court to void under §54960.1's literal …

[premortem] …isted became its own trap
**Likelihood: M  Severity: M**

What went wrong: Because the Harbor Yards appeal hadn't yet been decided when the cure-and-correct demand arrived, there arguably was no completed "action" under Gov. Code § 54952.6 for § 54960.1 to reach in the first place (verify whether pre-decision serial deliberation without a vote/collective commitment qualifies as "action taken" for § 54960.1 purposes, or whether the correct vehicle was inju…

[steelman] …ppened. That is the opposite of the classic serial-meeting harm, where three votes are locked up in private before the public ever gets to speak.

This matters independently for remedy. "Action taken" is a defined term (§ 54952.6): a collective decision, commitment, or promise by a majority of the body, or an actual vote. The cure-and-correct mechanism of § 54960.1 exists to nullify action taken in violation of specified open-meeting provisions.…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Cal. Gov. Code § 54952.6","note":"Section exists and defines \"action taken\" under the Brown Act as a collective decision, a collective commitment/promise by a majority to decide, or an actual vote of the body upon a motion, proposal, resolution, order, or ordinance. All three document snippets use it correctly for this narrow definitional purpose, correctly tying it to the § 54960.1 cure-and-correct remedy which applies only to \"action taken\" in violation of enumerated sections.","source_urls":["https://california.public.law/codes/ca_gov't_code_section_54952.6","https://law.justia.com/codes/california/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54952-6/","https://firstamendmentcoalition.org/handbook/california-brown-act-primer/"]}

data.note: Section exists and defines "action taken" under the Brown Act as a collective decision, a collective commitment/promise by a majority to decide, or an actual vote of the body upon a motion, proposal, resolution, order, or ordinance. All three document snippets use it correctly for this narrow definitional purpose, correctly tying it to the § 54960.1 cure-and-correct remedy which applies only to "action taken" in violation of enumerated sections.
